# MAIN ARTICLE # Full length article # Enhanced peroxidase-like activity of CuO/Pt nanoflowers for colorimetric and ultrasensitive Hg2+ detection in water sample Qi Lian a , Han Liu a , Xuefang Zheng a,b, ⁎ , Xiaoming Li a , Fengjie Zhang a , Jing Gao b, ⁎⁎ - a College of Chemical Engineering, Hebei Normal University of Science and Technology, Qinhuangdao066601, PR China b School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, 8Guangrong Road, Hongqiao District, Tianjin300130, PR China # A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Colorimetric sensor Peroxidase CuO/Pt nanoflowers Mercuric ions Inhibition # 1. Introduction Over the past years, nanozymes have received tremendous attention because of their favorable catalytic activity, easy synthetic protocol, excellent environmental stability, and cheapness compared with natural enzymes. Nanozymes showed excellent application in manyfields including waste water treatment, medical diagnosis, and biosensing [1 -4]. In order to enhance the catalytic activity of nanozymes, a variety of functional nanomaterials have been extensively investigated including noble metals [5,6], metal framework (MOF) [7], transition metal oxides [8 -13], carbon nanomaterials [14 -16], and functional polymers [17]. However, the catalytic activity of these nanomaterials were not enough high, which restrict the potential applications. To promote the catalytic efficiency of nanozymes, the fabrication of hybrid nanomaterials was developed [18 -23]. For example, Zhu et al., prepared Co3O4@CeO2nanotubes as efficient peroxidase-like mimics for the detection of ascorbic acid (AA). The peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the as-prepared Co3O4@CeO2nanotubes was much higher than that of individual Co3O4nanofibers and CeO2nanotubes alone due to # A B S T R A C T A general method for the preparation of Pt doped CuO/Pt nanoflowers (CuO/Pt NFs) for peroxidase mimicking has been demonstrated via hydrothermal method. Pt ions could be reduced to Pt nanoparticles by using the ascorbic acid (AA) as reducing agent and Pt ions were uniformly doped in the CuO nanoflowers by using the bovine serum albumin (BSA) as the stabilizer. The obtained CuO/Pt NFs exhibited a higher peroxidase-like catalytic activity than individual CuO and Pt nanoparticles alone, which could be ascribed to their synergistic effect. In the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), the CuO/Pt NFs could catalyze the oxidation of chromogen3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to a blue color product. The peroxidase activity of CuO/Pt NFs was selectively inhibited by Hg2+ due to form the CuO/Pt-Hg trimetallic amalgam. However, the inhibition was not affected by other metal ions. Therefore, a novel colorimetric biosensor was developed for sensitive detection of mercuric ions by using CuO/Pt NFs. This method exhibited a wide response linear range, low limit of quantifi cation values, excellent sensitivity and selectivity for detection of Hg2+ in various ground water samples, which was verified with CVAAS. With the advantages of rapid response, simple operation, naked-eye observation and low cost, the colorimetric CuO/Pt NFs sensor showed bright prospects for detection of Hg2+ in various water samples and public drinking water distribution systems. the synergistic effect between the two components [24]. Other hybrid nanomaterials system had been developed based on the synergistic enzyme-like catalytic effect, such as Fe@Co3O4hollow nanocages [25], Au-loaded nanoporous Fe2O3nanocubes [26], Ag/MOF hybrid [27], Ag@carbon dots [28], FePt/graphene oxide nanocomposites [29], FeMnO3@polypyrrole nanotubes [30], reduced graphene oxide/CuS hybridnanosheets [31] etc. Recently, another effective route was reported that doping with suitable heteroatoms could enhance the enzyme-like catalytic activity of nanomaterials [32]. For instance, Gao et al [33] prepared Mo-doped Co3O4 (Mo-Co3O4) nanotubes endow an enhanced peroxidase-like activity compared with individual Co3O4nanotubes alone, which was attributed to the additional reaction oxygen species by the doping elements. However, the enhancement multiplier was still relatively low. Therefore, it was a great challenge to improve the peroxidase-like activity by preparing novel types of heteroatom-doped nanomaterials. Copper oxide (CuO) was one of representative transition-metal oxides with a narrow band gap (1.2eV), possessing unique chemical and physical properties as well as significant applications [34] in ⁎ Correspondence to: X. Zheng, College of Chemical Engineering, Hebei Normal University of Science and Technology, Qinhuangdao066601, PR China. ⁎⁎ Corresponding author. E-mail addresses: 201611501002@stu.hebut.edu.cn (X. Zheng), jgao@hebut.edu.cn (J. Gao). Contents lists available at ScienceDirect # Applied Surface Science journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/apsusc Fig. 1. SEM images of CuO/PtNFs (A, B). TEM (C, D) and HRTEM (E, F) images of CuO/PNFs. Insert in (C) showed the size distribution of CuO/Pt NFs. Fig. 2. (A) The spectra of XRD pattern of CuO/Pt nanoflowers with different reaction time (2h, 4h, 6h, 8h10h, 12h, 14h and16h). (B) Nitrogen adsorptiondesorption isotherms of the obtained CuO/Pt nanoflowers. The inset showed the pore size distribution. catalysis, solar cells, sensors. Recently, CuO nanoparticles were reported to possess the good peroxidase-like properties, indicating promising the application in biosensingfield [35]. However, it was still a challenge to enhance the peroxidase-like activity of CuO nanoparticles. Recently, CuO-based hybrid nanomaterials had been researched with synergistic effect [36]. For example, graphene quantum dots-copper oxide nanocomposites (GQDs/CuO) could be used as an effective peroxidase mimics to detect glucose and H2O2, which exhibited a much higher peroxidase-like activity than individual CuO nanoparticles alone [36]. However, there was no report to promote the peroxidase-like activity of CuO nanoparticles by a simple doping approach with suitable heteroatoms. Nowadays, contamination of the environment with heavy metal ions had been an urgent problem in our daily life [37]. Mercury ions (Hg2+ ) were toxic and caused numerous concerns, which could lead to the permanent damage of organs, nervous, tissues, and renal system of humans [38]. So far, a variety of methods [39] had been developed for mercury ions detection, including inductively coupled plasma-mass (ICPMS), gas chromatography, X-ray absorption spectroscopy. However, the traditional methods for detection of mercury ions were complicated and time consuming. Thus, developing a good method for rapid, economical and sensitive detection of mercury ions in environmental water samples has become the current focus. In this work, platinum (Pt) doped CuO nanoflowers (CuO/Pt NFs) were prepared for the colorimetric detection of mercury ions. The nanoflowers shaped CuO nanoparticles with more active catalytic sites and large surface area were beneficial to promote the higher peroxidase-like activity. Moreover, the doping with transition metal ions Fig. 3. High resolution XPS spectra of the typical CuO/Pt NFs. (A) CuO2p, (B) Pt4f, (C) Cu LM, (D) O1s. contributed to the enhanced peroxidase-like activity. The catalytic activity of peroxidase mimicking of CuO/Pt NFs was much higher than that of pristine CuO nanoparticles. In the presence of Hg2+ , the peroxidase activity of CuO/Pt NFs was obviously inhibited due to form the CuO/Pt-Hg trimetallic amalgam. Therefore, a selective and sensitive colorimetric assay for toxic detection of mercuric ions was developed. This work exploited simple operation, rapid response, low cost and naked-eye observation the colorimetric CuO/Pt NFs sensor, which hold great potential for mercuric ions detection in various water samples and public drinking water distribution systems. # 2. Experimental # 2.1. Chemicals and materials H2PtCl6, ascorbic acid (AA), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, Mw=40,000g/mol), Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, NH3·H2O (25%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ethyl alcohol, bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co. (Shanghai, China). 3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, 99.0%), hydrogenperoxide (H2O2, 30wt %) and metal salts of AR grade were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, including Hg2+ , Li + , K + , Na + , Ca2+ , Mn2+ , Co2+ , Mg2+ , Cu2+ , Zn2+ , Ni2+ , Ba2+ , Cd2+ , Sr2+ , Al3+ , Pb2+ , Fe3+ , Cr3+ , and Sn4+ . All other chemical reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. # 2.2. Preparation of CuO/Pt nanoflowers (CuO/Pt NFs) Typically, 0.94g Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (25mL, 0.16M) and0.26g PVP were dispersed in20mL ultrapure water, which was vigorously stirred for10min. Then, 0.5mL NH3·H2O (25%) and0.75mL (1M) sodium hydroxide (NaOH) aqueous solution were added in the aforementioned solution and stir well. 750 μ L of H2PtCl6 (40mM) and ultrapure water (3mL) were mixed together. Bovine serum albumin (BSA, 2.5mg with1.0mL ultrapure water), which was used as the stabilizer, was transferred into the above solution. The mixture was stirred in an ice-water bath for10min. And then, 5mL (0.1M) of AA solution was added into the above mixture dropwise under vigorous stirring. Then, the dispersion was transferred into a100mL teflon lined stainless autoclave and sealed, which was maintained at120 °C for6h. After being cooled to room temperature, the crude products were centrifuged at8000rpm for15min and supernatant was removed. Thefinal dark brown products were washed three times with ethanol and dried overnight in a vacuum at80 °C. # 2.3. Characterizations X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained on a Bruker D8Discover diffractometer (Germany) with Cu K α irradiation ( λ =1.5406 Å) operated at40kV and40mA. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was carried out on a SU8010scanning electron microscopy. High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were carried out on FEI Tecnai G2and F20microscopes at200kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed by using a Thermo Scientific Escalab250Xi system. Ultraviolet-visible light (UV -vis, UV2401PC) absorption spectra and absorbance measurements were recorded on a UV-2401PC spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Instrument, USA). And nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms were carried out on the Quantachrome Autosorb-IQ. # 2.4. Peroxidase activity of CuO/Pt nanoflowers In the presence of H2O2, the peroxidase activity of CuO/Pt Fig. 4. (A) The UV -vis absorption spectra of (a) TMB + H2O2 + CuO/Pt NFs, (b) H2O2 + TMB, (c) CuO/Pt NFs + TMB, and (d) TMB. The inset showed the corresponding photographs. (B) Catalytic oxidation of TMB by CuO/Pt NFs at different reaction time. (C) The time-dependent UV -vis absorption spectra of TMB-H2O2system catalyzed by CuO/Pt NFs. Inset: The typical photography of TMB-H2O2-CuO/Pt NFs before and after reaction for10min. (D) The UV -vis absorption spectra of TMB-H2O2system catalyzed by CuO NPs, Pt NPs, and CuO/Pt NFs, respectively. TMB: 0.5mM; H2O2: 6mM; NaAc-HAc buffersolution: pH4.0; time: 10min. The concentration of CuONPs, PtNPs, CuO/Pt NFs: 0.1mM. The inset showed the corresponding photographs. nanoflowers was investigated by catalytic oxidation of TMB as the peroxidase substrate. The substrate TMB could be catalyzed to generate a blue oxidation TMB solution (oxTMB), which was evaluated the absorbance at650nm by UV -vis. The mechanism of catalytic activity was also investigated. The reaction was carried out with0.1mM CuO/ PtNFs, 0.5mM TMB and6mM H2O2at the temperature of35 °C, and reaction time of10min in acetate buffer of pH4.0. The total reaction volume was1.0mL. The effect of reaction conditions of pH (2 -9), temperature (25 -60 °C), concentration of CuO/Pt NFs (0.02 -0.2mM), and TMB (0.1 -1.0mM) on the peroxidase activity was analyzed. Kinetic assays were performed with varied TMB (0.1 -1.8mM) or H2O2 (0.25 -6mM) concentration under optimal conditions. The kinetic parameters were calculated according to the Michaelis-Menten equation [40]: where v stands the initial velocity of the reaction, Vmax represents the maximal reaction velocity, [ S ] is the concentration of substrate (TMB or H2O2), Km is the Michaelis constant. And the kinetic parameters Vmax and Km could be achieves by the following Lineweaver-Burk plot [41]. # 2.5. Mercuric ion (Hg2+ ) detection The colorimetric detection of mercuric ions was carried out with0.1mM CuO/Pt NFs, 0.5mM TMB, 6mM H2O2and different concentration of Hg2+ at the temperature of35 °C and reaction time of10min in acetate buffer of pH4.0. Firstly, CuO/Pt NFs were added into the acetate buffer, and then the stock solution of Hg2+ was added to the above solution, and thefinal concentration of Hg2+ was varied from0to3.5 μ M. The aforementioned solution was incubated for3min at room temperature, followed by addition of the stock solution of TMB and H2O2to initiate the reactions. The solution was incubated for10min at35 °C and the absorbance at650nm was monitored by UV -vis spectrophotometer. To investigate the specificity of the proposed colorimetric strategy, different metal ions were added to the stock solution to CuO/Pt NFs to reach afinal concentration of100 μ M, including Li + , K + , Na + , Ca2+ , Mn2+ , Co2+ , Mg2+ , Cu2+ , Zn2+ , Ni2+ , Ba2+ , Cd2+ , Sr2+ , Al3+ , Pb2+ , Fe3+ , Cr3+ , and Sn4+ , which were used as the target analytes to be compared with mercuric ions. # 2.6. Analysis of different water samples Different water samples of MilliQ water, ground water and tap waterfiltered with0.45 μ M syringefilters were involved to develop method based on CuO/Pt NFs sensor peroxidase inhibition by Hg2+ . The concentration of Hg2+ was analyzed by the method described above and the results evaluated from the ground water samples were made a comparison with Electronic Corpo-ration of India Limited, MA5800E cold vapour atomic absorptions pectrometer (CVAAS) (Hyderabad, India) [39]. Fig. 5. The effect of pH (A and B), temperature (C and D), CuO/Pt NFs concentration (E and F), and TMB concentration (G and H) on the catalytic oxidation of TMB by CuO/Pt NFs. Values were mean ± SD for n =3. Inset: the corresponding photographs. # 3. Results and discussion # 3.1. Characterizations of CuO/Pt nanoflowers (CuO/Pt NFs) The morphologies of CuO/Pt NFs were investigated by SEM (Fig. 1A and B). The SEM showed that the CuO/Pt NFs with an average diameter of300 -350nm were dispersed (Fig. 1A and C). And the CuO/Pt NFs architectures were constructed by several intersectional nanoplates with a thickness of about1.9nm, and the distance between the nanoplates was about20 -30nm (Fig. 1B). The specific peroxide-like activity could be given by theflower-shaped morphology with large specific surface areas. To further investigated the morphology and crystallographic features of the CuO/Pt NFs, TEM and HRTEM images were recorded (Fig. 1C -F). Fig. 1C and D images of the CuO/Pt NFs architectures were constructed by intersectional nanoplates. The selected-area HRTEM was displayed in Fig. 1E and F, which exhibited the CuO/Pt NFs were Fig. 6. Catalytic mechanism and steadystate kinetic assay of CuO/Pt NFs. The velocity of the reaction was investigated by0.1mM CuO/Pt NFs in pH4.0, reaction temperature of35 °C, with incubated time of10min. The error bars were measured three times. (A) The concentration of TMB wasfixed of0.5mM and the H2O2concentration was varied. (B) The concentration of H2O2wasfixed of6mM and the concentration of TMB was varied. (C and D) Double reciprocal plots of activity of CuO/ PtNFs with the concentration of one substrate (H2O2or TMB) fi xed and the other varied. polycrystalline microstructure composing of some extremely nanocrystallites with different orientations. The well resolved2D lattice fringes with plane distances of0.250nm and0.232nm were attributed to the (002) and (111) planes, which was reflected by XRD with the strong peak of the (002) and (111) facets [42]. Furthermore, the lattice spacing distance was between0.2650nm and0.1962nm, belonging to the single Pt [43], which was reflected by XRD with the peaks of the (111) and (220) facets (Fig. 1F). The diameter of Pt was about5nm. The lattice spacing distance of CuO and Pt indicated the formation of the nanostructures of the CuO/Pt NFs. The XRD analysis of CuO/Pt NFs was investigated with different reaction time (from2h to16h) in Fig. 2A. The refraction peaks at32.3°, 36.5°, 48.6°, 53.3°, 61.6°, 65.8.3°, 66.2°, 75.3°, 82.9° could be indexed to the (110), (002), ( -202), (020), (202), ( -113), (022/ -311), (004/ -222), (222) in the obtained CuO (ICDD-PDF Card No. 01-089-5897) [42]. And the peaks at39.2° and67.6° were attributed to the (111) and (220) planes of Pt [44]. And no characteristic peaks of Cu2O and Cu(OH)2were found in the spectra, indicating the highly purity of the obtained CuO/Pt NFs. With time went by, the peaks were increased obviously. Nanoparticles were always interesting in biomimetic chemistry with many unique advantages, including facile preparation, high operational stability, low cost, and tunable catalytic activity [45,46]. The BET surface area was208m2g -1 . Due to the large specific surface area and more active sites endowed by the3D superstructures (Fig. 2C and D), the CuO/Pt NFs were given a higher probability of collision between active molecules trapped inside [47]. Fig. 3showed the XPS spectrum of the CuO/Pt NFs and the corresponding Gaussian curefi tting spectra of Survey XPS analysis of Cu2p, Pt4f, Cu LM2, and O1S could be seen in the spectra, respectively. The high-resolution Cu2p spectra of the CuO/Pt NFs could be obviously deconvoluted into four components (Fig. 3A). The gap between the level of933.7eV and953.7eV was20eV, which was agreed perfect with that of standard spectrum of CuO [48,49]. The peaks at933.7eV and953.7eV were attributed to the spectrum of Cu2p3/2and Cu2p1/ Fig. 7. (A) and (B) Schematic diagram showed the inhibition of CuO/Pt NFs peroxidase-like activity by mercuric ions. (C) The effect of different metal ions (100 μ M) and mercuric ions (1 μ M) on the peroxidase activity of CuO/Pt NFs. 2. The typical shake-up satellite peaks were clearly observed (962.2eV, 953.6eV, 943.9eV, 941.0eV, and933.7eV), which was identified the existence of Cu (II) [50]. The peaks at71.4eV (Pt4f7/2), 74.9eV (Pt4f5/2), 77.29eV (Pt4f5/2) were attributed to Pt0and Pt II species, respectively [51]. The peaks at564.9eV, 569.1eV and574.1eV were attributed to the Cu LM2 (Fig. 3C). The peaks of O1s at529.7eV, 531.0eV and532.2eV (Fig. 3D) were attributed to the binding energy value of Cu e O, O e H and O e O, respectively [52,53]. The peaks shown in Fig. 3indicated that the metallic state of Pt and CuO were existed in the sample of CuO/Pt NFs. # 3.2. Peroxidase-like activity of CuO/PtNFs In order to evaluate the peroxidase activity of the synthesized CuO/ Pt NFs, the catalytic oxidation of the chromogen TMB (3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine) with CuO/PtNFs was monitored by the spectrophotometrically in the presence of H2O2. As can be seen in Fig. 4A, the CuO/PtNFs could catalyze the oxidation of TMB to oxTMB (blue product) in the presence of H2O2in pH4.0buffer solution. The blue product could exhibit a strong absorbance at650nm, which was the typical color reaction of the peroxidase-like activity and was exhibited by the many nanoparticles and enzyme horseradish peroxidase [54]. Subsequently, the reaction systems were restricted with TMB in the absence of CuO/Pt NFs or H2O2. There were no blue products formed, indicating that the TMB and H2O2were indispensable to exhibit intrinsic peroxidase-like activity. The reaction time was monitored by UV -vis in Fig. 4B and C. The absorbance at650nm was increased with the time increased. At10min, the absorbance reached the maximum value and remained constantly. Therefore, reaction time of10min was determined to the optimal reaction time for the further experiments. Fig. 4D showed the catalytic behaviors of CuO NPs, Pt NPs, and CuO/Pt NFs, respectively. Under the same conditions, the obtained CuO/Pt NFs exhibited the highest catalytic activity, and the corresponding color changes of oxidized TMB were showed in Fig. 4D. The highest enzyme mimicking activities could be ascribed to the synergistic effects of both the large surface area and the active sites of the CuO/Pt nanoflowers, rather than the simple physical combination of two components [44]. To optimize the catalytic reaction conditions, we also investigated the absorbance changes of the oxidized TMB with important parameters (Fig. 5), such as pH (2 -9), temperature (25 -60 °C), CuO/Pt NFs concentration (0.04 -0.20mM), and TMB concentration (0.1 -1.0mM). The effects of pH and temperature were measured by varying the pH from2to9 (Fig. 5A and B) and the reaction temperature from25to60 °C (Fig. 5C and D) under the optimal conditions. The relative activity (%) remained high and stable when the reaction temperature increased to60 °C and the pH increased to9.0. The peroxidase activity reached the maximum values, when the pH was about4.0and the reaction temperature was35 °C, which was determined to the optimal conditions. The effects of CuO/Pt NFs concentration (Fig. 5E and F) and TMB concentration (Fig. 5G and H) on the peroxidase activity were involved under the optimal conditions. When the concentration of CuO/Pt NFs and TMB were0.1mM and0.5mM, the peroxidase activities reached the optimum values, respectively, and then constant. Therefore, 0.1mM CuO/Pt NFs and0.5mM TMB were selected as the optimal parameters. To investigate the kinetic mechanism of the peroxidase activity of CuO/Pt NFs, the steady-state kinetic parameters of the oxidation reaction catalyzed by CuO/Pt NFs were chosen by changing the concentration of H2O2or TMB, which were followed the typical MichaelisMenten behavior with the substrates of H2O2and TMB (Fig. 6A and B). The oxidized products concentration of TMB by CuO/PtNFs was calculated by the Lambert-Beer law using the values ε TMB = 39,000M -1 (at650nm). The maximum initial velocity ( Vmax) values and the Michaelis-Menten constant ( Km) were calculated by Lineweaver-Burk plot (Fig. 6C and D). The Km values for TMB and H2O2were calculated to be0.33mM and0.75mM, respectively. And the Vmax values towards TMB and H2O2were27.86 × 10 -8MS -1and12.88 × 10 -8MS -1 , respectively. By comparing with the natural enzyme HRP ( Km = 0.434) and other metal oxide nanoparticles, CuO/Pt NFs had a relatively lower Fig. 8. (A) UV -vis absorption spectra of the solution in presence of different concentration of Hg2+ (0 -3.5 μ M) based on CuO/Pt NFs. (B) Plot of the absorbance as a function of the concentration of mercuric ions varied from0to3.5 μ M. (C) The absorbance change of the solution was linear with the concentration of Hg2+ from0.1nM to700nM. (D) The calculated limit of detection (LOD) (S/N = 3). (E) Corresponding color photographs of the solution corresponding to (A). Km value and a higher Vmax value (Table1). The Km was considered to be an indicator of enzyme affinity to substrates. The smaller Km, the stronger is the appetency between the substrate and enzyme [55]. With the lower Km and higher Vmax, CuO/Pt NFs endowed excellent catalytic affinity to TMB and H2O2, which improved the activity binding sites and the peroxidase like activity. Fig. 6C and D illustrated the double-reciprocal plots of the peroxidase like activity of CuO/Pt NFs by changing the concentration of H2O2atfixed TMB concentration or vice versa. The mechanism of catalysis activity of CuO/Pt NFs was followed the ping-pang mechanism, which was consistent with HRP and other nanoparticles [56]. More detailed explanation could be that, during the catalysis procedure, the CuO/Pt NFs could bind and react with thefirst substrate, and then react with the second substrate after releasing thefirst substrate [57,58]. Therefore, the lines were presented to be parallel lines with different concentration of substrate. # 3.3. Sensitivity and selectivity of CuO/Pt NFs towards Hg2+ Fig. 7A and B exhibited the inhibition scheme of CuO/Pt NFs peroxidase-like activity by Hg2+ . The CuO/Pt NFs could catalyze H2O2to produce OH radicals. And then the OH radicals oxidize the TMB to oxidase TMB with blue color, which had a strong absorbance at650nm. After addition of Hg2+ , the redox reaction was drastically inhibited. This phenomenon could be explained by two reasons. Thefirst one was that the CuO/Pt NFs showed much higher catalytic activities than that of pure CuO/Pt NFs and Pt NPs, mainly due to the synergistic effects of the bicomponent nanosystem (Fig. 4D). The second and the more important point was attributed to the strong electron transfer happened between Hg2+ and the CuO/Pt NFs surface, resulting in the decrease of active sites available for oxidation of TMB. There was a small gap between the standard electrochemical potential of Hg2+ /Hg (E° = 0.85V) and Pt4+ /Pt (E° = 0.758V) [61], resulting in the difficulty of the redox reaction. However, the Pt nanoparticles was different from the bulk metal, resulting in the Pt4+ /Pt nano was much lower than the Pt4+ /Pt (E° = 0.758V) [39,41]. Thus, the potential difference between Hg2+ / Hg and Pt4+ /Pt nano became much higher, and the redox reaction occurred easily. The Pt0of the CuO/Pt NFs could catalyze the Hg2+ to Hg0 , simultaneously, which could react with CuO/Pt NFs to form amalgam of CuO/Pt-Hg. Thus, the intrinsic peroxidase activity of CuO/ Pt NFs was dramatically restrained because of formation of CuO/Pt-Hg alloy by amalgamation [41,62]. Fig. 9. Plots showing the absorbance at650nm of Hg2+ in different water samples of A) MilliQ water, B) tap water, C) ground water.

To investigate the selectivity and sensitivity of CuO/Pt NFs for Hg2+ , the sensor was challenged with different metal ions, such as Li + , Na + , K + , Ca2+ , Mn2+ , Co2+ , Mg2+ , Cu2+ , Zn2+ , Ni2+ , Ba2+ , Cd2+ , Sr2+ , Al3+ , Pb2+ , Fe3+ , Cr3+ , and Sn4+ (100 μ M), which was100folder greater than that of Hg2+ (1 μ M) (Fig. 7C). The involved metal ions (100 μ M) could not restrain the peroxidase activity of CuO/Pt NFs with the relative activity (%) about96 -100%. However, with the

The relative activity (%) was calculated to about6.8% (Fig. 7C), and the blue color of the product was changed to be colorless (Fig. 7D). Therefore, the specific CuO/Pt-Hg interaction offered an exceptional selectivity for Hg2+ , by comparing with other metal ions [62]. # 3.4. Colorimetric detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solutions In order to evaluate the CuO/Pt NFs peroxidase enzyme modulating activity of Hg2+ , a colorimetric assay for Hg2+ detection was constructed under the optimal conditions, which employed the chromogenic substrate TMB and CuO/Pt NFs to catalyze TMB-H2O2color reaction. As illustrated in Fig. 8A and B, with a raise of the concentration of Hg2+ from0to3.5 μ M, the absorbance peaks decreased gradually at650nm, indicating that the CuO/Pt NFs could be used for the quantitative detection of Hg2+ . It was significant to note that a good linear relationship between the concentration of Hg2+ and the absorbance from0.1nM to700nM with R2 =0.996 (Fig. 8C). The calculated limit of detection (LOD) was as low as0.049nM according to the S/N = 3rule (Fig. 8D), which was lower than the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) toxicity level of Hg2+ (10nM). With a raise of the concentration of Hg2+ from0to3.5 μ M, there was a reduction in the color intensity from blue to colorless (Fig. 8E), suggesting that the CuO/Pt NFs-based Hg2+ quantitative analysis strategy was feasible. To investigate the versatility of the above colorimetric strategy, different water samples were involved to detect the concentration of Hg2+ based on the inhibition assay by the CuO/Pt NFs sensor (Fig. 9). The sensing of Hg2+ concentration was carried out under the optimal conditions. With a raise of the concentration of mercuric ions, the absorbance at650nm was decreased gradually, and the color of the solution was faded from blue to colorless which could be observed by naked eyes obviously. As can be seen from Fig. 9, the reduction exhibited a good linear relationship in the concentration range of0.1nM to700nM of mercuric ions of MilliQ water (Fig. 9A), tap water (Fig. 9B), and ground water (Fig. 9C). The calculated limit of quantifi cation (LOQ) of MilliQ water, tap water and ground water were0.036, 0.049, and0.12, respectively (Table2). It was significant to note that CuO/Pt NFs sensor could act as an effective peroxidase for sensitive and selective colorimetric assay of mercuric ions in different water samples, without interference of other metal ions [38]. And the limit of detection based on the CuO/Pt NFs sensor was better than other assay methods [41]. To test the accuracy of proposed assay method to detect Hg2+ concentration, a spike recover experiments was developed based on the CuO/Pt NFs sensor on ground water samples. The results obtained by the proposed assay method were made a comparison with standard CVAAS method (Table3). According to the date in Table3, there was no significant difference between the two methods. Therefore, the proposed colorimetric assay method based on CuO/Pt NFs sensor of Hg2+ analysis strategy could be developed in actual analysis for real ground water samples. Colorimetric assays were proposed in this analytical method duo to the advantages of sensitivity, low cost, simplicity, and practicality. The proposed assay method for detection of Hg2+ was compared with other reported colorimetric methods, with the sensitivity, linear range and LOD (Table4). The proposed colorimetric assay provided a sensitivity optical Hg2+ sensor by naked eyes (Fig. 8) with lower LOD (0.049nM) and wider linear range (0.1 -700nM) than that of other nanoparticles for detection of Hg2+ . # 4. Conclusions In summary, Pt doped CuO NFs as efficient peroxidase mimics were successfully synthesized. In the presence of H2O2, the CuO/Pt NFs endowed with a relatively best characteristic peroxidase-like property. The catalytic kinetic of CuO/Pt NFs followed a typical MichaelisMenten mechanisem, representing a prominent affinity towards TMB ( Km0.33mM) and H2O2 ( Km0.75mM). The mechanism of catalysis activity of CuO/Pt NFs followed the ping-pang mechanism. Based on the down regulating activity of Hg2+ towards the peroxidase activity of the CuO/Pt NFs, a colorimetric assay method with extremely sensitive and selective response towards mercuric ions detection was developed. Furthermore, the Pt doped CuONFs showed broad applications to sensitively detect Hg2+ with wide linear response range of0.1 -700nM, and low LODs of0.036nM (MilliQ sample), 0.049nM (tap sample), and0.12nM (ground water sample). This work offered a universal doping strategy to significantly enhance the peroxidase-like properties, which expand their applications in colorimetric sensing, public water distribution systems, and environmental monitoring. # Notes The authors declare no competingfinancial interest. # Acknowledgment This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21306039, 21276062, 21276060, and21576068), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province, China (B2016202027, B2015202082, B2018407041, and B2017202056), Hebei High level personnel of support program, China (A2016002027), the Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin City, China (16JCYBJC19 800). # References 1.
